file_name: Cartocci et al. - 2025 - Real or synthetic Dermatologist agreement on synthetic vs. real melanoma and pattern recognition.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a Generative Adversarial Network (specifically StyleGAN3-T) to generate synthetic melanoma images for dermatological evaluation. The AI model was trained on dermoscopic images of melanoma to create synthetic data, which was then assessed by dermatologists for realism and pattern recognition.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a blinded expert evaluation study where dermatologists assessed synthetic vs. real melanoma images generated by StyleGAN3-T. It includes specific methods (training GANs, participant recruitment, Likert scale ratings), quantitative results (classification accuracy, p-values, Fleiss' Kappa), and a conclusion based on these new findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Real or Synthetic? Dermatologist Agreement on Synthetic vs. Real Melanoma and Pattern Recognition
brief_summary: This study evaluated the realism and diagnostic utility of synthetic melanoma images generated by StyleGAN3-T compared to real dermoscopic images. Seventeen board-certified dermatologists participated in a blinded assessment, classifying 25 synthetic and 25 real images and rating them on quality and realism using a Likert scale. The overall classification accuracy was 64%, with real images consistently rated higher in image quality, skin texture, and visual realism (p < 0.001). Dermatologists significantly under-detected critical diagnostic patterns like the blue-white veil and shiny white streaks in synthetic images, indicating structural fidelity gaps. Inter-rater agreement for distinguishing real from synthetic images was low (Fleiss’ kappa 0.183), though accuracy improved to 74% among high-confidence evaluations. The findings suggest that while synthetic images show promise for research and education, expert validation remains essential to address limitations in structural detail and diagnostic pattern recognition.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused exclusively on melanoma. StyleGAN3-T was trained on a dataset of dermoscopic images of melanoma (referencing HAM10000 and other melanoma datasets). The experiment involved 25 synthetic melanoma images mixed with 25 real melanoma images for dermatologist evaluation.

all_task_types.details: The study utilized a Generative Adversarial Network (StyleGAN3-T) trained on dermoscopic images of melanoma to generate 25 synthetic melanoma images. The primary AI task was image synthesis to create data for subsequent expert evaluation.

datasets_used.name: StyleGAN3-T Training Dataset (Melanoma Dermoscopic Images)
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic images of melanoma used to train the StyleGAN3-T model. The paper references external sources for this data, specifically the HAM10000 dataset and a patient-centric dataset for identifying melanomas using clinical context.

use.training: Used to train the StyleGAN3-T Generative Adversarial Network with adaptive discriminator augmentation and transfer learning.
use.other: Served as the source for generating synthetic images.

datasets_used.name: Evaluation Dataset (Synthetic and Real Melanoma Images)
datasets_used.description: A mixed dataset of 50 dermoscopic images consisting of 25 synthetic melanoma images generated by StyleGAN3-T and 25 real melanoma images, used for blinded expert evaluation by dermatologists.

use.testing: Used for blinded assessment by 17 board-certified dermatologists to classify image origin (real vs. synthetic) and evaluate diagnostic patterns/quality.
use.other: Used for expert validation of synthetic image realism and diagnostic utility.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Likert scale ratings (image quality, skin texture, visual realism, color realism)
Pattern recognition rates (presence of dermoscopic patterns)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To perform categorical comparisons of dermoscopic pattern recognition rates and qualitative dimension ratings between real and synthetic images.
To measure inter-rater agreement for the classification of real versus synthetic images and for the recognition of specific dermoscopic patterns.
To measure inter-rater agreement for the classification of real versus synthetic images and for the recognition of specific dermoscopic patterns.
To assess subjective dimensions including image quality, skin texture, visual realism, and color realism on a 7-point scale.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in the distribution of qualitative dimension ratings (image quality, skin texture, visual realism, color realism), confidence in classification, or recognition rates of specific dermoscopic patterns between real and synthetic melanoma images.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in the distribution of qualitative dimension ratings, confidence in classification, or recognition rates of specific dermoscopic patterns between real and synthetic melanoma images.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: Real images were consistently rated higher than synthetic images across all qualitative dimensions and confidence levels (p < 0.001). Specific dermoscopic patterns such as the blue-white veil, shiny white streaks, and multicomponent pattern were significantly more frequently recognized in real images compared to synthetic ones (p < 0.001). Conversely, irregular pigmented blotches were significantly more frequent in synthetic images (p < 0.001). These results indicate statistically significant differences in expert perception and pattern recognition between real and synthetic images.

StyleGAN3-T (Synthetic Images).overall_classification_accuracy: 56% (of synthetic images correctly classified as synthetic)

Real Melanoma Images (Ground Truth).overall_classification_accuracy: 73% (of real images correctly identified as real)

Evaluation Dataset (Synthetic vs Real Me.notes: Real images were consistently rated higher than synthetic images across all qualitative dimensions (image quality, skin texture, visual realism) and confidence levels (p < 0.001). Inter-rater agreement for classification was low (Fleiss’ kappa 0.183). Pattern recognition agreement was weak (kappa < 0.3 for most features). Synthetic images significantly underrepresented critical diagnostic patterns like the blue-white veil and shiny white streaks, while overrepresenting irregular pigmented blotches compared to real images (p < 0.001 for all listed pattern differences).

training_details.dataset: Dermoscopic images of melanoma (referencing HAM10000 and patient-centric datasets)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used during the training of the StyleGAN3-T model on dermoscopic images of melanoma.

conclusions: Synthetic melanoma lesions generated using StyleGAN3-T demonstrate visually convincing features and were frequently perceived as real, yet consistently underperformed compared to real images in diagnostic quality and structural detail.
Participants often struggled to distinguish synthetic from real lesions, particularly when realism ratings were medium to high.
Critical diagnostic patterns, such as the blue-white veil and shiny white streaks, were significantly underrepresented in synthetic images.
These limitations were reflected in the lower classification confidence and weaker inter-rater agreement.
The study highlights the potential of synthetic data to approach realism levels sufficient for research and educational use.
Qualitative validation by dermatologists is essential to benchmark the readiness of synthetic images for real-world medical applications.
As generative models continue to evolve, expert evaluation should remain a key component of validation pipelines to ensure clinical and pedagogical reliability.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small sample size of only 25 synthetic and 25 real images, which may restrict the generalizability of the findings.
Inter-rater agreement for distinguishing real from synthetic images was low (Fleiss’ kappa 0.183), indicating significant variability among dermatologists in their assessments.

Synthetic images significantly underrepresented critical diagnostic patterns such as the blue-white veil (13.8% vs 29.1%, p < 0.001) and shiny white streaks (7.9% vs 22.6%, p < 0.001), highlighting structural fidelity gaps.
Synthetic images overrepresented irregular pigmented blotches (45.0% vs 30.9%, p < 0.001) compared to real images, potentially introducing bias in pattern recognition.

Real images were consistently rated higher in image quality, skin texture, and visual realism (all p < 0.001), suggesting current synthetic models do not fully match expert expectations for diagnostic utility.

The study relied on a single GAN architecture (StyleGAN3-T) trained on melanoma-specific datasets (HAM10000 and patient-centric data), limiting the assessment of broader generative model capabilities.
Expert validation remains essential, as automated metrics may not align with human perception and diagnostic expectations for subtle visual cues in medical imaging.

Classification accuracy improved with dermatologist experience (59% for low-experience vs 71% for high-experience) and confidence (74% for high-confidence evaluations), suggesting that expert skill influences the detection of synthetic artifacts.
